coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots combined with the oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce4+ for ratiometric fluorescent detection of Ce4+ and alendronate sodium
coredata.dc:creator: Xia, Meng
Zhao, Xian-En
Sun, Jing
Zheng, Zhenjia
Zhu, Shuyun
coredata.dc:description: A simple ratiometric fluorescent sensing system for Ce4+ and alendronate sodium (ALDS) detection is developed with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) combined with the oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce4+. As oxidase-mimicking, Ce4+ can catalyze the oxidization of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) directly without the requirement of H2O2. The oxidation product (oxOPD) not only exhibits a strong emission at 562 nm but also quenches the fluorescence of GQDs at 444 nm significantly through inner filter effect (IFE). Due to its strong oxidative ability, Ce4+ can also oxidize ALDS. Thus, ALDS can inhibit the oxidization of OPD by Ce4+. The fluorescence at 444 nm can be protected from quenching while that at 562 nm decreases. Thus, a ratiometric fluorescent strategy for quantitative analysis of Ce4+ and ALDS has been developed. The ratiometric sensing platform exhibits a high sensitivity with the detection limits of 1.0 μM and 33 nM for Ce4+ and ALDS, respectively. This strategy can detect Ce4+ and ALDS in practical samples with satisfactory recoveries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Graphene quantum dots
Oxidase-mimicking
Ce4+
Alendronate sodium

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric detection of Ce4+ and ALDS
Investigation on the fluorescence quenching mechanism
Quantitative detection of Ce4+ and ALDS

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of GQDs-based ratiometric fluorescent assays for Ce4+ and ALDS.
The low-magnification (A) and high-magnification (B) TEM image, AFM image (C), and corresponding height profile (D) of GQDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs (a), GQDs + Ce4++ OPD (b), GQDs + Ce4+ (c), GQDs + OPD (d). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra GQDs (a), GQDs + ALDS (b), GQDs + Ce4++ OPD (c), GQDs + Ce4++ ALDS + OPD (d). [GQDs] =3.2 mg·mL-1, [Ce4+] =100 μM, [OPD] =2.24 mM, [ALDS] =80 μM.
(A) UV-vis absorption spectra of GQDs, GQDs + oxOPD, oxOPD, GQDs + Ce4+, and GQDs + OPD. (B) UV-vis spectrum of oxOPD (Abs), fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectrum of GQDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the GQDs-OPD system in the presence of different concentrations of Ce4+. (B) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratios I 562/I 444 versus Ce4+ concentration. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the GQDs-Ce4+-OPD system in the presence of different concentrations of Ce4+. (D) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratios I 562/I 444 versus ALDS concentration. [GQDs] =3.2 μg·mL-1; [Ce4+] =80 μM. [OPD] =2.24 mM. Error bars were estimated from three replicate measurements.

(A) Ratiometric fluorescent responses of GQDs-OPD system to some common anions and metal ions. From 1 to 23 is Cl-, CO3 2-, NO3-, SO4 2-, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ce3+, Ag+, Cu2+, Ce4+, Ag+ + I-, Cu2+ + EDTA, respectively. The concentrations of other interferents are 100 μM. [Ce4+] =80 μM; [I-] =100 μM; [EDTA] =100 μM. (B) Ratiometric fluorescent responses of GQDs-Ce4+-OPD system to some coexisting interferents. From 1 to 18 is control, ALDS, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, NO3-, SO4 2-, HPO4 2-, PO4 3-, uric acid, ascrobic acid, starch, lactose, dextrin, mannitol, maltose, and magnesium stearate, respectively. [uric acid] =2.0 μM; [ascrobic acid] =1.0 μM; the concentration of other analyte is 100 μM. Error bars were estimated from three replicate measurements.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for the determination of Ce4+.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The inclusion complex of β-cyclodextrin with benzoguanamine.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different optical methods for the detection of ALDS.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots combined with the oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce4+ for ratiometric fluorescent detection of Ce4+ and alendronate sodium

Key Laboratory of Food Processing Technology and Quality Control in Shandong Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Key Laboratory of Food Processing Technology and Quality Control in Shandong Province, Taian, 271018, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple ratiometric fluorescent sensing system for Ce4+ and alendronate sodium (ALDS) detection is developed with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) combined with the oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce4+. As oxidase-mimicking, Ce4+ can catalyze the oxidization of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) directly without the requirement of H2O2. The oxidation product (oxOPD) not only exhibits a strong emission at 562 nm but also quenches the fluorescence of GQDs at 444 nm significantly through inner filter effect (IFE). Due to its strong oxidative ability, Ce4+ can also oxidize ALDS. Thus, ALDS can inhibit the oxidization of OPD by Ce4+. The fluorescence at 444 nm can be protected from quenching while that at 562 nm decreases. Thus, a ratiometric fluorescent strategy for quantitative analysis of Ce4+ and ALDS has been developed. The ratiometric sensing platform exhibits a high sensitivity with the detection limits of 1.0 μM and 33 nM for Ce4+ and ALDS, respectively. This strategy can detect Ce4+ and ALDS in practical samples with satisfactory recoveries.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the rare earth elements, cerium has been widely used in ceramics, luminescence, catalysis, glass, and television sets [1]. They can be accumulated in human body leading to the formation of thrombosis in the lung and liver [2]. Due to its strong oxidative ability, Ce4+ has a destructive effect on metabolism of RNA and DNA, gene expression, and signal transduction [3]. Many methods have been applied for monitoring Ce4+, including X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [4], inductively coupled plasmatic optical emission spectrometry [5], and spectrophotometric method [6]. Fluorescence has become a powerful analytical tool because of its high sensitivity and selectivity [7]. Some organic fluorescent dyes have been designed and synthesized for monitoring Ce4+ [8–10]. Nevertheless, these fluorophores usually need complex synthesis procedure and consuming purification process. Thus, it is still significant to design other fluorescent probes for monitoring Ce4+.

Alendronate sodium (ALDS) is an aminobiphosphonate medicine [11]. It can promote bone formation as well as osteoblast proliferation and maturation. Thus, the osteoblast apoptosis can be inhibited. ALDS has been regularly used to treat osteoporosis in postmenopausal women and glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis [12]. Moreover, it has been used for the treatment of Paget’s disease and hypocalcemia caused by malignancy. Nevertheless, ALDS exhibits toxicity to soft tissues [13]. Therefore, it is important to detect ALDS in human body fluids as well as in related medicines. Various techniques have been used for monitoring ALDS such as electrochemical methods [14], chromatography [15], and spectroscopy [16]. Fluorescence analysis methods have drawn great attention because of their facile operation, rapid response and high sensitivity [17]. Since ALDS has no fluorophores, its fluorometric determination represents a challenge. Valizadeh’s group developed a fluorometric determination of ALDS based on the derivation of ALDS with o-phthalaldehyde [18]. Jia and coworkers proposed a “turn-on” fluorescence method for the detection of ALDS with DNA-templated silver nanoclusters [19]. These two methods either need complicated derivation step or expensive DNA. Hence, it is necessary to design an easy-operated and cost-effective method to detect ALDS.

As a rising star of carbon-based nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have drawn great interesting owing to their unique intrinsic properties of photochemical stability, low cytotoxicity, and strong fluorescence signals [20,21]. As the most promising fluorescent probes, GQDs have been utilized for designing various chemical and biochemical assays [22–24]. Most of these fluorescent nanosensors are single-wavelength-based detecting mode through either “turn-on” or “turn-off” strategy. Such detecting mode can be easily interfered with experimental conditions such as optical path length, instruments, environmental factors and autofluorescence in complicated biosystems. Thus, the sensitivity and accuracy are limited.

Generally, ratiometric fluorescent probes have two separated emission bands. The presence of analytes can cause obvious fluorescence intensity changes from these two emission bands. Compared with single signal output, the ratiometric strategy can not only avoid the potential interferences from background fluorescence but also improve sensitivity and accuracy [25]. A variety of ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors have been established based on GQDs [26–34]. For example, Su’s group established a ratio system to detect bleomycin based on GQDs@gold nanoclusters assembly [26]. They also developed a ratiometric fluorescent assay for p53 gene based on GQDs and berberine [27]. Li and coworkers established a ratiometric system for monitoring tetracylines by using GQDs and Eu3+ as fluorescence reporters [28]. Chen’s group applied folic acid encapsulated GQDs for ratiometric pH sensing [29]. Li and his coworkers designed a GQDs-based ratiometric probe for sensing RNA [30]. Poon et al. developed a versatile ratiometric nanoprobe to detect trypsin with GQDs and coumarin derivative [31]. The ratiometric systems based on GQDs and 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) were also applied for the determination of horseradish peroxidase [32], DNA [33], and Ag+ [34]. To the best of our knowledge, ratiometric fluorescent determination of Ce4+ and ALDS based on GQDs has not been reported.

Recent study shows that Ce4+ exhibits a powerful oxidase-like activity [35] and thus is expected to oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into oxOPD directly. The absorption spectrum of oxOPD overlapped with the emission spectrum of GQDs. Inner filter effect (IFE) can occur between them. Taken together, we propose a GQDs-based ratiometric fluorescence method for stepwise determination of Ce4+ as well as ALDS. As presented in Scheme 1 , the fluorescence intensities of oxOPD at 562 nm increase greatly as the concentrations of Ce4+ increase. Meanwhile, those at 444 nm corresponding to GQDs decrease simultaneously due to IFE. The oxidation of OPD induced by Ce4+ is inhibited by ALDS because of the redox reaction between Ce4+ and ALDS. As a result, the fluorescence intensity corresponding to oxOPD decreases significantly with a simultaneous fluorescence increase of GQDs. Thus, a ratiometric sensor for Ce4+ and ALDS has been demonstrated. The GQDs-based ratiometric sensor can detect Ce4+ and ALDS in practical samples with satisfactory recoveries.

GQDs (20.0 mg mL-1) were provided by Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech. Co. Ltd. Alendronate sodium trihydrate (ALDS) was provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. The cerium sulfate tetrahydrate (CeS2O8), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), uric acid, ascorbic acid, acetic acid, sodium acetate, starch, lactose, dextrin, stearate, mannitol, and maltose were purchased from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). Alendronate sodium tablets were provided by Shijiazhuang Pharmaceutical Group Ouyi Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd. (Batches 152180601). Uric samples were provided by the healthy volunteers. HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 M) was used through the experiments. All reagents were of analytical grade, and used as received.

UV-vis absorption spectra were performed with a UV-3600 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded by F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed with a Philips CM200 FEG microscope. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were performed with a Multi Mode Nanoscope III controller atomic force microscope (Veeco, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were measured with an ESCALAB 250xi spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection of Ce4+ and ALDS
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 339 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 7.0), 56 μL OPD (20 mM), and 25 μL Ce4+ with different concentrations were mixed thoroughly. After incubation for 1 h at room temperature in dark, 80 μL GQDs (20.0 mg mL-1) were added into the above mixture. After 5 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded. For analysis of ALDS, 25 μL varied concentrations of ALDS was incubated with 20 μL Ce4+ (2.0 mM) for 5 min at room temperature. Then, 319 μL HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 7.0) and 56 μL OPD (20 mM) were added to the above mixture. After reaction for 60 min, 80 μL GQDs were added to the mixture and reacted for 5 min. The spectra were measured. The ratiometric fluorescence signal variations at I 562/I 444 were utilized for the detection of Ce4+ as well as ALDS.

For practical detection of Ce4+, water samples from Weishan Lake were measured using our developed assay. Prior to analysis, water samples were filtered through a 0.45 μm filtered membrane. After that, they were analyzed by standard addition method according to the general procedure.

For the detection of ALDS in tablet, a tablet was ground into powder. An amount equivalent to 12.21 mg ALDS was dissolved with 100 mL distilled water. The solution was filtered to obtain a stock solution with 100 μM ALDS. Finally, the solution was analyzed by the proposed method.

For analysis of ALDS in human urine, fresh uric samples were diluted for 1000 times before analysis. 25 μL uric samples were premixed with standard ALDS solution for 5 min. And then the mixture was analyzed according to the general procedure.

Fig. 1 A shows the TEM image of GQDs. It can be seen that GQDs are spherical with an average size of 4.2 nm. Moreover, GQDs are well-separated from each other (Fig. 1B). The thickness of GQDs was investigated by AFM imaging (Fig. 1C). As presented in Fig. 1D, the thickness is about 3.5 nm corresponds to about 3 graphene layers [36].

Surface analysis of GQDs was studied with XPS as well as FT-IR spectroscopy. The surface elements of GQDs was analyzed by using XPS with the results presented in Fig. S1A. There are two elements in GQDs: carbon and oxygen. As shown in Fig. S1B, the high-resolution C1s spectrum at 284.1, 284.9, 286.3, 288.0, and 288.8 eV is assigned to C-C, C = C, C-O, C = O, and COO-, respectively. Fig. S1C shows the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs. Absorption peaks at 3450 and 1080 cm-1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of O-H and C-O-C, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1586 and 1398 cm-1 indicates the presence of carboxylate. These results demonstrate the existence of hydroxyl as well as carbonyl group. Fig. S1D shows that GQDs has an absorption peak at 330 nm caused by the π-π* electronic transition [37]. GQDs possess the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths at 366/444 nm, respectively. The quantum yield (QY) of GQDs is calculated to be 9.1% using quinine sulfate as reference and the details are presented in Electronic Supporting Information.

The feasibility for ratiometric detection of Ce4+ was examined. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence of GQDs at 444 nm decreases in the presence of Ce4+ and OPD (curve a,b). Meanwhile, a new emission peak at 562 nm appears. The UV-vis spectra were also investigated with the results shown in Fig. S2. It can be seen that there is an apparent absorption peak at 430 nm for OPD after reaction with Ce4+. These results indicate that the oxidation of OPD can be triggered by Ce4+. Nevertheless, the fluorescence of GQDs changes slightly in the presence of either Ce4+ (curve c) or OPD (curve d). As a result, this method can be used for ratiometric detection of Ce4+.

The performance of this strategy for ratiometric detecting ALDS was assessed with the results shown in Fig. 2B. It can be seen that ALDS has negligible influence on the fluorescence of GQDs (curve a,b). When ALDS is introduced to the GQDs-Ce4+-OPD system, the fluorescence of oxOPD decreases obviously while that of GQDs increases simultaneously (curve c,d). The influences of ALDS on spectral properties of Ce4+-OPD system were also investigated (Fig. S3). Not only the absorption at 430 nm but also the fluorescence at 562 nm decrease obviously, indicating that ALDS inhibits the oxidative ability of Ce4+ towards OPD. Accordingly, a ratiometric fluorescent strategy can be designed to detect ALDS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on the fluorescence quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: In general, static quenching, dynamic quenching, and IFE can induce fluorescence quenching phenomenon. The static quenching is attributed to the generation of a nonfluorescent ground-state complex, usually followed by changes of absorption spectra [38]. The UV-vis absorption spectra of GQDs, GQDs + oxOPD, oxOPD, OPD, GQDs + Ce4+, and GQDs + OPD were investigated (Fig. 3 A). The results show that oxOPD has a negligible influence on the absorption spectrum of GQDs. No other new absorption peak generates, indicating no new complexes are formed. Thus, the static quenching is excluded in this sensing strategy.

The dynamic quenching mechanism includes both Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET). They occur in the excited-state followed by changes of the fluorescence lifetime [39,40]. Fig. S4 shows that the fluorescence lifetime of GQDs and GQDs with oxOPD is 2.12 and 2.07 ns, respectively. The presence of oxOPD has negligible influence on the fluorescence lifetime of GQDs. The zeta potentials for GQDs and oxOPD are -30.4 and -7.5 mV, respectively. There is electronic repulsion between GQDs and oxOPD. Their distance is hardly less than 10 nm. The FRET between GQDs and oxOPD can be neglected. In a word, the dynamic quenching can be excluded.

IFE is a very effective fluorescence quenching mechanism. IFE needs a spectral overlap between the absorption band of the quencher and the excitation and/or emission band of the fluorophore [41]. Moreover, IFE does not change the lifetime of fluorophore [42]. Fig. 3B shows that the absorption band of oxOPD significantly overlaps with the emission spectrum of GQDs. All these results indicate that fluorescence quenching of GQDs is caused by oxOPD through IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of Ce4+ and ALDS
ce:section.ce:para: Before quantitative analysis of Ce4+, the detection conditions were optimized including the reaction time, OPD concentration, and pH value. The fluorescence intensity ratio I 562/I 444 is used as standard. Fig. S5A shows the effect of reaction time between Ce4+ and OPD on the response to Ce4+. The fluorescence intensity ratio I 562/I 444 increases gradually and then changes slightly with the increasing reaction time. The reaction between Ce4+ and OPD almost completes within 60 min. Thus, 60 min is set for reaction between Ce4+ and OPD.

Secondly, the concentration of OPD can influence the total amount of the generating oxOPD, thus influencing the response sensitivity towards Ce4+. Fig. S5B represents the effect of OPD concentration on the ratio I 562/I 444. The ratio I 562/I 444 increases quickly and then reaches balance when the OPD concentration is 2.24 mM. Therefore, 2.24 mM OPD is used in the following experiments.

pH is an important factor that influences the oxidation ability of Ce4+. In view of the instability of Ce4+ in alkaline conditions, the influence of pH value in the range of 3.5-7.5 on the response sensitivity towards Ce4+ was investigated. As shown in Fig. S5C, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 562/I 444 increases remarkably with increasing pH value when the pH is less than 5.5 and then remains constant in the pH range of 5.5-7.5. In the strong acidic medium, the amino groups of OPD are protonated, which inhibit the oxidation by Ce4+. Moreover, the fluorescence intensities of GQDs change slightly in the pH range of 5.5-7.5 (Fig. S5D), indicating that GQDs are very stable within such pH range. All these results show that Ce4+ exhibits the same oxidase-like activity in the pH range of 5.5-7.5. Therefore, this strategy exhibits a good response towards Ce4+ in such wide pH range. In view of the subsequent practical application, the buffer with pH 7.0 is chosen for the next experiments.

The fluorescent response of the proposed ratiometric sensor for Ce4+ was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensities at 562 nm increase systematically while those at 444 nm decrease as Ce4+ concentrations increase from 0 to 700 μM. The fluorescence intensity ratio I 562/I 444 increases with the increasing concentrations of Ce4+, which reaches a plateau when the concentration of Ce4+ increases to 700 μM. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 562/I 444 is linear response to the Ce4+concentration in the range from 5 to 100 μM. The fitted linear relationship is described as I 562/I 444 = 0.1065 + 0.01985 C, R 2 = 0.9942. The detection limit for Ce4+ is 1.0 μM based on 3 Sb/slope, where Sb is the standard deviation for ten blank measurements. The sensitivity of our method is lower than those of most reported methods listed in Table 1 .

Ascribed to its strong ability, Ce4+ can oxidize bisphosphonates [47]. The redox reaction between Ce4+ and ALDS can inhibit the oxidation of OPD, leading to the decline of fluorescence intensity at 562 nm. Therefore, GQDs-Ce4+-OPD system can also be applied for ratiometrically fluorescent sensing ALDS. As presented in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence intensities at 562 nm decrease while those at 444 nm increase as ALDS concentrations increase from 0 to 300 μM. The fluorescence intensity ratio I 562/I 444 decreases with the increasing concentrations of ALDS, which reaches a plateau when the concentration of ALDS increases to 300 μM. As shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 562/I 444 is linear response to the ALDS concentration from 0.01 to 1.0 and from 1.0 to 100 μM, respectively. The regression equations are I 562/I 444 = 1.6698 - 0.2798 C (R 2 = 0.9875) and I 562/I 444 = 1.3623 - 0.01144 C (R 2 = 0.9965), respectively. The detection limit for ALDS reaches as low as 33 nM based on 3 Sb/slope, where Sb is the standard deviation for ten blank measurements. In comparison with other optical methods, our method exhibits higher sensitivity (Table 2 ).

The selectivity of this ratiometric strategy was investigated prior to practical applications. Fig. 5 A shows the selectivity of this ratiometric fluorescent platform for Ce4+. It is found that the common anions and metal ions such as Cl-, CO3 2-, NO3-, SO4 2-, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+ and Ce3+ have negligible influence on the fluorescent intensity ratios of I 562/I 444. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity ratio of I 562/I 444 increases remarkably after the introduction of Ce4+, Ag+ and Cu2+ into the GQDs-OPD system, respectively. Cu2+ as well as Ag+ can also oxidize OPD, thus interfering the detection of Ce4+ [53]. Nevertheless, the addition of EDTA and I- can keep from the interferences of Cu2+ and Ag+, respectively. Fortunately, the introduction of either EDTA or I- has slightly influence on the detection of Ce4+ (Fig. S6).

The selectivity of GQDs-Ce4+-OPD system for ALDS was also studied. As presented in Fig. 5B, I 562/I 444 decreases remarkably upon the addition of ALDS. In contrast, I 562/I 444 changes slightly by introducing the common interferents including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, NO3-, SO4 2-, HPO4 2-, PO4 3-, uric acid, ascrobic acid, starch, lactose, dextrin, mannitol, maltose, and magnesium stearate. This indicates that the common species in pharmaceutical samples exhibit negligible influence on ALDS detection.

To evaluate the potential applications of this method, the real samples were analyzed. Weishan Lake samples were used for detection of Ce4+ by the standard addition method. As illustrated in Table S1, Ce4+ is not detected in the water samples. After spiking with various concentrations of Ce4+, water samples are analyzed by using this ratiometric fluorescent strategy. The recoveries of Ce4+ are in the ranges of 94.8%-105.6%. In addition, the results obtained using this ratiometric fluorescent method were statistically compared with those obtained by using a reported fluorescent method [54]. The data in Table S1 shows that the two methods produce close results. All these results indicate that this ratiometric fluorescent strategy can possess the great potential for analyzing Ce4+ in environmental water samples.

This ratiometric fluorescent method was applied for analyzing ALDS in the commercially available Alendronate sodium tablet which is labeled to contain 70 mg of the analyte per tablet. The results obtained using the ratiometric fluorescent method were statistically compared with those obtained by using a reported spectrophotometric method [48]. The data in Table S2 shows that the two methods produce close results, both of them are congruent with the label claim of the dosage form. Moreover, the calculated t- and F-values are less than the tabulated ones, confirming accuracy and precision at 95 % confidence limits. This ratiometric fluorescent system was also applied to detect ALDS in human uric samples. Various ALDS standard solutions were added into the diluted uric samples with the results shown in Table S3. The recovery tests show that the satisfactory recoveries of 93-105.6% are obtained. All these results indicate that this strategy possess great potential in analyzing ALDS in practical samples.

In summary, GQDs-based ratiometric fluorescent assays for Ce4+ and ALDS has been developed in combination with the oxidase-like activity of Ce4+. Using Ce4+ as oxidase-mimicking, OPD can be oxidized directly to generate oxOPD, which quenched the fluorescence of GQDs via IFE. Based on the redox reaction between Ce4+ and ALDS, a convenient and sensitive ratiometric fluorescent assay for ALDS was achieved. As enzyme mimics, Ce4+ can be available directly from commercial reagents avoiding the tedious preparation, and complex purification. As demonstrated, the developed assay has been successfully applied to detect Ce4+ in water samples and ALDS in human uric samples as well as in tablets. The unequivocal recognition mechanism and simple response process make this ratiometric strategy great potential in practical applications.

Meng Xia: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Xian-En Zhao: Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Jing Sun: Data curation. Zhenjia Zheng: Software, Validation. Shuyun Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interest or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is kindly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21775088).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128321.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A turn-on fluorescent chemosensor with the aggregation-induced emission characteristic for high-sensitive detection of Ce ion
Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.H. Wang, X.L. Pan, Z. Peng, Y.H. Zhang, P. Liu, Z.X. Cai, B. Tong, J.B. Shi, Y.P. Dong, A turn-on fluorescent chemosensor with the aggregation-induced emission characteristic for high-sensitive detection of Ce ion, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 367 (2018) 351-356.

Int. J. Nanomed.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Nemmar, S. Al-Salam, S. Beegam, P. Yuvaraju, B.H. Ali, The acute pulmonary and thrombotic effects of cerium oxide nanoparticles after intratracheal instillation in mice, Int. J. Nanomed. 12 (2017) 2913-2922.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cerium oxide nanoparticles scavenge nitric oxide radical (NO)
Chem. Commun.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Total reflection X-ray fluorescence determination of rare earth elements in mineral water
Mosc. Univ. Chem. Bull.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.V. Oskolok, O.V. Monogarova, N.V. Alov, Total reflection X-ray fluorescence determination of rare earth elements in mineral water, Mosc. Univ. Chem. Bull. 72 (2017) 10-14.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Meng Xia received her bachelor degree in Pharmaceutical Engineering from Qufu Normal University in 2018, and now is a postgraduate student in Analytical Chemistry at Qufu Normal University. Her research interests focus on the synthesis and analytical application of carbon nanomaterials.

Xian-En Zhao received his PhD from Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology in 2009, and now is working at Qufu Normal University as a professor in Analytical Chemistry. His current research interests include biosensors, mass spectrometry analytical methodology, and pharmaceutical analysis.

Jing Sunreceived his PhD from Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology in 2007, and now is working at Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology as an associate professor in Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources. Her current research interests include quality control of Tibetan medicine and chemical analysis of resources.

Zhenjia Zheng received his PhD from College of Food Science and Engineering in Shandong Agricultural University at 2018. And now he is working at Shandong Agricultural University as an associate professor. His current research interests include food processing, food active ingredients analysis and analytical chemistry.

Shuyun Zhu received her PhD from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry in 2012, and now is working at Qufu Normal University as an associate professor in Analytical Chemistry. Her current research interests include nanomaterials, as well as biosensors based on DNA and enzymes.
